perturbation.original_text: Accounting statements and, if applicable, royalty payments for products sold will be rendered by each party quarterly within sixty (60) days after the expiration of each calendar quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Accounting statements and, if applicable, royalty payments for products sold will be rendered by each party at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific timeframe (quarterly within 60 days) gives the impression that accountings can be provided less frequently and without a fixed schedule. This introduces ambiguity and potential for delayed or less transparent reporting, which can harm the other party.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-2-306(1) requires good faith in performance and enforcement of contracts, including output, requirements and exclusive dealing agreements. Removing the specific accounting period could be interpreted as acting without good faith by providing inadequate sales information, particularly where royalties depend on it.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies a clear timeframe for rendering accounting statements and royalty payments (quarterly, within 60 days). The changed text removes this specific timeframe, replacing it with 'at their discretion.' This omission contradicts the implied duty of good faith as it introduces ambiguity and potential for delayed reporting, impacting the other party, and therefore goes against Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-2-306(1) as cited in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Either party, at its sole expense, upon at least thirty (30) days written notice (and not more than once in respect of any accounting period) will have the right to inspect the other party's books regarding the obligations hereunder for a period of two (2) years from the date on which any statement is rendered.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may inspect the other party's books regarding the obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a 30-day notice period, limits inspections to once per accounting period, and provides a two-year window for auditing. Removing these constraints potentially eliminates the right to audit within a reasonable time, and the right to receive enough notice before auditing. This disadvantages the parties involved by removing clarity in auditing responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract or duty. Removing the specific conditions around auditing, like the need for advance notice, could be construed as acting in bad faith, hindering the ability of a party to verify the accuracy of royalty payments. Without the original text, it removes clarity in the contract and could be deemed to not acting in good faith in enforcing the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-1-304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. The original text included specific conditions like a 30-day notice, limiting inspections, and a two-year audit window, ensuring clarity and fairness in auditing. By omitting these conditions, the changed text removes these protections. This removal, as described in the Law Explanation, could be seen as not acting in good faith, because it hinders a party's ability to verify royalty payments due to lack of clarity.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto warrants, represents, covenants and agrees that it has the right and power to enter into this Agreement, to grant the rights herein granted by it, and to perform the services agreed to be performed by it hereunder, and that no materials, ideas or other properties furnished or designated by it is subject to any restriction whatsoever, or is violative of the rights of any person, firm or corporation, including, without limitation, contract rights, copyrights and rights of privacy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to enter into this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This clause originally offers a comprehensive warranty covering the rights to enter the agreement, grant rights, perform services, and guarantees that provided materials don't infringe on other rights like contract, copyright, or privacy. The modified text significantly reduces this warranty to only cover the right to enter the agreement. This omission exposes the other party to potential legal risks associated with copyright infringements. By removing the original warranty, the agreement becomes less protective, increasing the liability risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-2-312 implies a warranty of title and against infringement in contracts. Although this section technically refers to 'goods', similar principles apply to intellectual property licenses and representations. By severely weakening the original warranty, the contract provides less assurance against potential copyright or patent infringement claims related to the 'Product' being developed and co-marketed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits significant warranties, which contradicts the original contract's protection against rights infringements. The Law Explanation suggests a connection to warranty principles, and while Snippet 1 discusses the sale of 'goods', it highlights the concept of warranty against infringement, stating that a seller warrants goods to be delivered 'free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement'. The original text provides a broader warranty. By removing this, the modified contract provides less assurance against such claims, creating a legal loophole and increasing liability risks, fitting the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.
